sentences.base_language: Tanaka Kenji is a programmer and works a lot during the week.
sentences.target_language: 田中健二はプログラマーで、週の間はよく働きます。
sentences.romanized: Tanaka Kenji wa puroguramā de, shū no aida wa yoku hatarakimasu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜で** after a noun connects two clauses, like 'being ~ and also': プログラマーで、よく働きます (is a programmer and works a lot). **週の間** (shū no aida) = during the week — **の間** (no aida) expresses 'during/within' a time period.
**は** (wa) as the topic particle signals what the sentence is 'about.' Here it marks both 田中健二 and 週の間 as topics, creating a contrast: 'as for Kenji... as for the week.'

sentences.base_language: On Saturday morning, he woke up at seven.
sentences.target_language: 土曜日の朝、七時に目が覚めました。
sentences.romanized: Doyōbi no asa, shichiji ni me ga samemashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**目が覚める** (me ga sameru) = to wake up, lit. 'eyes awake' — a natural Japanese phrase where the eyes are the grammatical subject. Compare: **起きる** (okiru, to get up) focuses on the body rising, while **目が覚める** focuses on the moment of waking.
**に** after a specific time = 'at' that time: 七時に (at seven), 三時に (at three). For relative times like 朝 (morning) or 今日 (today), **に** is not used — the noun stands alone.

sentences.base_language: He was very hungry and wanted to go to the grocery store.
sentences.target_language: とてもお腹が空いていて、スーパーマーケットに行きたかったです。
sentences.romanized: Totemo onaka ga suite ite, sūpāmāketto ni ikitakatta desu.
literal: スーパーマーケットに
sentences.grammarNote:
**お腹が空く** (onaka ga suku) = to be hungry, lit. 'stomach becomes empty' — the stomach is the grammatical subject. Similarly: **喉が渇く** (nodo ga kawaku, to be thirsty, lit. 'throat dries').
**〜たかった** = wanted to do (past desire): **行く** → **行きたい** (want to go) → **行きたかった** (wanted to go). The **〜て** form in **空いていて** connects the two clauses: 'was hungry AND wanted to go.'

sentences.base_language: He washed his face, got dressed, and left home.
sentences.target_language: 顔を洗って、服を着て、家を出ました。
sentences.romanized: Kao wo aratte, fuku wo kite, ie wo demashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
The **〜て** form chains sequential actions: 洗って → 着て → 出ました (washed, then dressed, then left). Only the final verb is conjugated for tense. This is one of the most fundamental Japanese grammar patterns.
**家を出る** = leave home — **を** with movement verbs marks the place departed from: 家を出る (leave home), 部屋を出る (leave the room), 駅を出る (exit the station). Compare **に** which marks the destination.

sentences.base_language: The grocery store was nearby — only a ten-minute walk.
sentences.target_language: スーパーマーケットは近くにあって、歩いて十分しかかかりません。
sentences.romanized: Sūpāmāketto wa chikaku ni atte, aruite juppun shika kakarimasen.
literal: スーパーマーケットは
sentences.grammarNote:
**しか〜ない** = only (with a nuance of 'merely / no more than'): 十分しかかかりません (it takes only ten minutes). Compare: **だけ** (just/only, neutral) vs **しか〜ない** (only, with a limiting or modest nuance).
**歩いて** = on foot, lit. 'by walking' — the **〜て** form of a locomotion verb expresses the means of travel: 歩いて (on foot), 走って (by running), 電車に乗って (by train). Also note **あって** (aru + te) connecting existence to the next clause.

sentences.base_language: There he bought eggs, vegetables, and chicken.
sentences.target_language: そこで卵と野菜と鶏肉を買いました。
sentences.romanized: Soko de tamago to yasai to toriniku wo kaimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**で** after a place noun marks where an action takes place: そこで (there), スーパーで (at the supermarket), 公園で (in the park). Compare **に** which marks where something exists or a destination: 公園にいる (be in the park), 公園に行く (go to the park).
**と** between nouns = 'and' (exhaustive list): 卵と野菜と鶏肉 (eggs and vegetables and chicken — these specific items). Compare **や** (and so on) which implies a non-exhaustive list: 卵や野菜 (eggs, vegetables, and other things).

sentences.base_language: He also grabbed some fruit: apples and bananas.
sentences.target_language: りんごやバナナなどの果物もいくつか手に取りました。
sentences.romanized: Ringo ya banana nado no kudamono mo ikutsuka te ni torimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜や〜など** = 'things like ~ and ~' — used for non-exhaustive lists. **などの** modifies the following noun: りんごやバナナなどの果物 (fruit like apples and bananas).
**も** = also/too: 果物も (fruit too). **も** replaces は or が when adding an item: 野菜を買いました + 果物も (bought vegetables AND also fruit).
**いくつか** = some (several): **いくつ** (how many) + **か** makes it indefinite: いくつか (some number of). Similarly: いくら**か** (some amount), いつ**か** (sometime).

sentences.base_language: After finishing, he sent his friend a message.
sentences.target_language: 買い物が終わると、友達にメッセージを送りました。
sentences.romanized: Kaimono ga owaru to, tomodachi ni messēji wo okurimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜と** (conditional/sequential) = 'when/as soon as': 終わると (when/as soon as it ends). This **と** marks a natural consequence or immediate sequence — different from the quotation と or the list と.
**友達に送る** = send to a friend — **に** marks the recipient of giving/sending verbs: 友達にメッセージを送る (send a message to a friend), 先生に手紙を書く (write a letter to the teacher). The pattern: [receiver] に [item] を [verb].

sentences.base_language: His friend replied: "I'm waiting for you at the café!"
sentences.target_language: 友達は「カフェで待ってるよ！」と返事しました。
sentences.romanized: Tomodachi wa 'Kafe de matteru yo!' to henji shimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**と** after a quoted clause marks reported speech or a message: 「〜」と返事する (reply that ~), 「〜」と言う (say that ~). Japanese requires the full quote before **と**.
**待ってる** = short form of **待っている** (am waiting) — dropping the **い** from **〜ている** is extremely common in casual speech: 食べてる (is eating), 来てる (has come). **よ** at the end adds a light assertive emphasis: 'I'm waiting (you know)!'

sentences.base_language: Kenji left the bags at home and went to the café.
sentences.target_language: 健二は袋を家に置いて、カフェに行きました。
sentences.romanized: Kenji wa fukuro wo ie ni oite, kafe ni ikimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**置く** (oku) = to put/place/leave: 家に置く (leave at home). **に** marks the location where something is placed: テーブルに置く (put on the table), 棚に置く (put on the shelf).
**〜て form + 行く/来る** = go/come in order to: 買いに行く (go to buy), 会いに来る (come to meet). Here the **〜て** simply connects two sequential actions (left the bags, then went) — a core building block of Japanese narrative.

sentences.base_language: His friend Sakura was already sitting there, with a smile on her face.
sentences.target_language: 友達のさくらさんはもうそこに座っていて、顔に笑顔を浮かべていました。
sentences.romanized: Tomodachi no Sakura-san wa mō soko ni suwatte ite, kao ni egao wo ukabete imashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ている** = ongoing state or action: 座っている (is sitting). For verbs of posture (座る, 立つ, 寝る), **〜ている** describes the resulting state: 座っている (is in a seated state), 寝ている (is asleep). This is different from 'is sitting down' (座った) which emphasises the action.
**笑顔を浮かべる** = to show/wear a smile, lit. 'to float a smile' — **浮かべる** (ukaberu) is an expressive verb meaning to have something visible on one's face or mind: 涙を浮かべる (to have tears in one's eyes), 疑問を浮かべる (to show a look of doubt).

sentences.base_language: Sakura asked him: "How are you? You look tired."
sentences.target_language: さくらさんは「元気ですか？疲れているように見えますよ」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Sakura-san wa 'Genki desu ka? Tsukarete iru yō ni miemasu yo' to iimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ように見える** = look/appear to be ~: 疲れているように見える (you look tired), 嬉しそうに見える (you look happy). **ように** (yō ni) expresses manner or appearance: 'in a way that looks like ~'.
**元気ですか** = 'are you well/healthy?' — **元気** (genki) = energy/health/liveliness. It covers both 'are you healthy?' and 'how are you?' depending on context. Common response: 元気です (I'm fine), まあまあです (so-so).

sentences.base_language: Kenji sighed and said: "Work has me stressed — there's a lot of pressure."
sentences.target_language: 健二はため息をついて、「仕事でストレスを感じています。プレッシャーがたくさんあります」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Kenji wa tameiki wo tsuite, 'Shigoto de sutoresu wo kanjite imasu. Puresshā ga takusan arimasu' to iimashita.
literal: ストレスを感じています
sentences.grammarNote:
**ため息をつく** = to sigh, lit. 'to attach/give a sigh' — a fixed expression: **ため息** (tameiki, sigh) + **つく** (tsuku, to attach). Japanese has many fixed [noun + verb] idioms: 目を引く (catch the eye), 首を振る (shake one's head), 肩をすくめる (shrug).
**〜でストレスを感じる** = to feel stress from ~: **で** marks the cause or sphere: 仕事でストレス (stress from work), 試験でプレッシャー (pressure from the exam). **感じています** (kanjite imasu) = am feeling (ongoing state with **〜ている**).

sentences.base_language: Sakura said: "You need to relax! Let's go to the park for a little walk."
sentences.target_language: さくらさんは「リラックスしないと！一緒に公園を少し歩きに行きましょう」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Sakura-san wa 'Rirakkusu shinai to! Issho ni kōen wo sukoshi aruki ni ikimashō' to iimashita.
literal: if don't relax [you'll be in trouble]
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ないと（いけない）** = must do / have to do: リラックスしないと (you need to relax). This is a truncated form of **〜ないといけない**. In casual speech the second half is dropped because it's understood: 行かないと！(I've gotta go!), 急がないと！(We need to hurry!).
**〜に行く** = go in order to do: 歩きに行く (go to walk/for a walk). The verb before **に** is in its **masu-stem** (pre-masu) form: 歩き (from 歩きます). **〜ましょう** = let's ~: volitional polite form.

sentences.base_language: After finishing their coffees, they went to the park together.
sentences.target_language: コーヒーを飲み終えてから、二人は一緒に公園へ向かいました。
sentences.romanized: Kōhī wo nomioe te kara, futari wa issho ni kōen e mukaimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**飲み終える** = finish drinking — **〜終える** (oeru) attaches to the masu-stem of a verb to express completing an action: 食べ終える (finish eating), 読み終える (finish reading), 書き終える (finish writing). Compare **〜始める** (start doing).
**〜てから** = after doing: 飲み終えてから (after finishing drinking). This emphasises sequence — first the coffee, then the park. Compare **〜た後で** (after having done), which is interchangeable in many cases. **へ** (e) marks direction of movement, similar to **に** but more literary.

sentences.base_language: The park air was fresh — Kenji took a deep breath and felt much better.
sentences.target_language: 公園の空気は新鮮で、健二は深呼吸して、ずっと気分がよくなりました。
sentences.romanized: Kōen no kūki wa shinsen de, Kenji wa fukukokyū shite, zutto kibun ga yoku narimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜くなる** = to become ~ (adjective change): よくなる (become better), 大きくなる (become bigger), 悲しくなる (become sad). This is how Japanese expresses becoming a new state: the adjective shifts to its **〜く** form + なる.
**気分** (kibun) = feeling/mood (in the moment): 気分がいい (feeling good), 気分が悪い (feeling unwell). Compare **気持ち** (kimochi) = feeling/sensation (more physical or emotional): 気持ちいい (feels good), 悲しい気持ち (a sad feeling). Both overlap but **気分** is more about overall mood.

sentences.base_language: They walked through the park for an hour and talked about everything.
sentences.target_language: 二人は一時間、公園を歩きながら、いろいろなことを話しました。
sentences.romanized: Futari wa ichijikan, kōen wo aruki nagara, iroiro na koto wo hanashimashita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ながら** = while doing (two simultaneous actions): 歩きながら話す (talk while walking), 音楽を聴きながら勉強する (study while listening to music). The **〜ながら** action is the secondary one — the main verb comes at the end.
**公園を歩く** = walk through the park — **を** with movement verbs marks the path traversed: 公園を歩く (walk through the park), 橋を渡る (cross the bridge), 道を走る (run along the road). This **を** is different from the direct-object **を**.

sentences.base_language: Walking home, Kenji's body felt a bit sore, but his spirits were very good.
sentences.target_language: 家に帰る途中、健二は体が少し痛かったですが、気分はとてもよかったです。
sentences.romanized: Ie ni kaeru tochū, Kenji wa karada ga sukoshi itakatta desu ga, kibun wa totemo yokatta desu.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜途中** (tochū) = on the way / in the middle of: 帰る途中 (on the way home), 食べている途中 (in the middle of eating). Used with both nouns and verbs: 帰り道に (on the way back — using the noun), 帰る途中に (on the way of returning — using the verb).
**〜が** at the end of a clause = 'but' (soft contrast): 痛かったですが、気分はよかった (the body ached but the mood was good). This **が** creates a gentle contrast, softer than **でも** (but, at the start of a new sentence).

sentences.base_language: He decided to go out with friends every weekend from now on.
sentences.target_language: これからは毎週末、友達と出かけることにしました。
sentences.romanized: Korekara wa maishūmatsu, tomodachi to dekakeru koto ni shimashita.
literal: 出かけることにしました
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ことにする** = to decide to do: 出かけることにしました (decided to go out). This is the key expression for a personal decision: 早く寝ることにした (decided to go to bed early), 野菜を食べることにする (am going to eat vegetables). Compare **〜ことになる** (it has been decided, by circumstances or others).
**これから** = from now on / from here: これからは (as for from now on) — **は** adds a sense of 'going forward.' **毎週末** (maishūmatsu) = every weekend: **毎** (mai) + any time word means 'every': 毎日 (every day), 毎朝 (every morning), 毎年 (every year).

sentences.base_language: That night, he fell asleep very quickly and slept wonderfully.
sentences.target_language: その夜、あっという間に眠りにつき、ぐっすり眠りました。
sentences.romanized: Sono yoru, atto iu ma ni nemuri ni tsuki, gussuri nemurimashita.
literal: in the blink of an eye
sentences.grammarNote:
**あっという間に** = in the blink of an eye, lit. 'in the time it takes to say ah' — a very common idiom for something happening extremely quickly. Use it freely: あっという間に終わった (it was over in a flash), 時間はあっという間に過ぎた (time flew by).
**ぐっすり** = soundly (sleep): a Japanese adverb (擬態語, gitaigo — a mimetic word) that describes the quality of deep sleep. Japanese has many such expressive adverbs: ぐっすり (sound sleep), ふわふわ (fluffy/floating), ドキドキ (heart pounding), キラキラ (sparkling). They are highly evocative and very common in everyday speech.
